data.title: Asante Net Sales Edge Down in February
data.description:
Asante reported February net sales of 991 million yen, slightly down by 0.4% from a year earlier, according to a Friday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

For the April 2024 to February 2025 period, the pest control and home maintenance firm recorded a year-on-year sales increase of 2.1%, with total revenue reaching 12.8 billion yen.

data.title: FTSE Straits Times Index Closes 0.1% Lower at 3914.48
Ceres Meets All Prime Market Listing Compliance
data.description:
Ceres has met all Prime Market listing maintenance standards as of Dec. 31, 2024, according to its Friday filing with the Tokyo bourse.

The company increased its number of shareholders to 10,584 and its market capitalization of tradable shares to 18.2 billion yen, exceeding listing criteria.

Efforts to comply included improving earnings per share through profit recovery, reducing capital costs via enhanced investor relations, and executing its Medium-Term Management Plan 2026.

Ceres shares shed nearly 4% at market close.

data.title: SSAB to Install Electric Arc Furnace at Swedish Site
data.description:
Steel producer SSAB (, SSAB-A.ST, SSAB-B.ST) plans to replace blast furnaces at its mill in Oxelösund, Sweden, with an electric arc furnace.

The company said Friday the mill will be the first to make a green transition in its Nordic production system. The new system is expected to be operational at the end of 2026.

Guangzhou Automobile Group Co Ltd :

data.title: Eurozone Bonds Could Underperform Due to Europe's Fiscal Spending — Market Talk
data.description: European government bonds are expected to underperform other markets given upcoming fiscal expenditures, Jupiter Asset Management's Vikram Aggarwal says in a note. "Jupiter AM was anticipating a loosening of the fiscal stance from the new German administration, the size of the fiscal package was surprising," the portfolio manager says. These changes are going to be one of the most important shifts in German economic policy since the second World War, he says. "To put this in context, this is the largest economy in Europe announcing fiscal expansion which could reach close to 20% of GDP." Given Germany's spending plans, Jupiter AM prefers U.S. duration and has an underweight stance in European duration. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

Daily performance is calculated by reference to the previous day NAV/share and VNAS (Vietnam All Share Index). Calendar month-to-date performance is calculated by reference to NAV/share and VNAS as of 28 Feb 2025. Calendar year-to-date performance is calculated by reference to NAV/share and VNAS at the previous calendar year end (31 Dec 2024). 1-year performance is calculated by reference to NAV/share and VNAS as of 07 Mar 2024. 3-year CAGR (compound annual growth rate) is calculated by reference to NAV/share and VNAS as of 07 Mar 2022. 5-year CAGR is calculated by reference to NAV/share and VNAS as of 07 Mar 2020. 10-year CAGR is calculated by reference to NAV/share and VNAS as of 07 Mar 2015. 15-year CAGR is calculated by reference to NAV/share and VNAS as of 07 Mar 2010. Since inception CAGR is calculated by reference to NAV/share as of 30 Jun 2006. VNAS is sourced from Bloomberg, which uses back-tested positions for VNAS for before 27 Jan 2014 as released by HoSE (Ho Chi Minh City Stock Exchange).

The Estimated Daily NAV stated in this announcement is based on estimated valuations and accordingly the Company accepts no responsibility for the accuracy of the Estimated Daily NAV figure given, and neither is any responsibility implied. The actual month end NAV in any month may vary from the Estimated Daily NAV provided in the relevant month. Accordingly, no reliance should be placed on the Estimated Daily NAV, and it has been provided for indicative purposes only.

data.title: Just Group's Balance Sheet to Help Stock Growth — Market Talk
data.description: Just Group's stock remains set for continued growth in the U.K. annuity market helped by its balance sheet, J.P. Morgan Cazenove analysts Farooq Hanif and Bingdi Fan say in a note. Although the analysts don't expect material positive underlying earnings revisions for 2025 as results for 2024 were close to consensus, they do see an upgrade on Solvency II ratio this year given its strong consensus beat of around 10 percentage points, the analysts say. "We also believe consensus may revise its dividend growth expectations up from here given the dividend per share beat," the analysts say. Shares are down 14% at 140.20 pence but are up 58% over the past 12 months. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0346 GMT - Bumrungrad Hospital's earnings may be hit by uncertainty over recovery of Middle East patients' volume, Maybank Securities (Thailand)'s Nontapat Sahakitpinyo says, noting its Middle East patient revenue fell 19% on year in 4Q. While the Thai hospital operator is optimistic about a recovery in Middle East patients, there are risks that patient volumes may not fully rebound and pricing adjustments, such as discounts, may be required to secure re-contracting, the analyst says. The brokerage cuts its 2025-2026 earnings forecasts for Bumrungrad Hospital by 7%-8%. It lowers the stock's target price to THB225.00 from THB250.00 and keeps a buy rating. Shares are 0.3% higher at THB178.50. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0025 GMT - Forsyth Barr's verdict on the unscheduled update from retirement village operator Oceania Healthcare: Worse than hoped, better than feared. Oceania signaled demand is soft, but not deteriorating while it remained positive around future cost savings. It also reflected confidence in its capital structure. "These messages are likely to alleviate concerns around its balance sheet that may have been priced into the stock, following Ryman Healthcare's capital raise," analyst Aaron Ibbotson said. "That said, the update was worse than hoped." Forsyth Barr trims its price target by 5.3% to NZ$0.90/share, while retaining an outperform call on Oceania's stock. Oceania is unchanged at NZ$0.68. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1150 GMT - Merck KGaA anticipates its research and development costs will normalize to industry-peer levels as its pipeline advances and recent deals mature, Chief Executive Officer Belen Garijo says in a media call. The life-sciences and electronics company's R&D spending was 2.3 billion euros in 2024, and it expects it to gradually increase in 2025. Garijo reaffirms Merck's strategy to bolster in-licensing--agreements to use another company's products--and consider smaller mergers and acquisitions to strengthen its pipeline. Merck is looking for new assets as one of its key growth drivers, multiple sclerosis drug Mavenclad, will face patent expiry in 2026. The German company's goal is to achieve a 50-50 split between internal and external innovation for future product launches, Garijo adds. Shares are up 1.6% at 139.30 euros. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

1118 GMT - Merck KGaA should see a minimal impact from U.S. import tariffs on its business, its chief executive officer, Belen Garijo, says in a call with reporters, alongside its annual results. Germany's Merck doesn't see an impact from U.S. tariffs imposed on Canada and Mexico, but a limited impact from those on China, Garijo says, emphasizing the regionalization of the company's supply chain. Shares are up 1.5% at 139.20 euros. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

1114 GMT - Merck KGaA's quantitative forecast instead of the usual qualitative outlook indicates greater management transparency, Stifel analysts say in a note. However, Stifel analysts still classify the German pharmaceutical-and-chemicals company's 2025 forecast, which is broadly in line with consensus, as relatively unambitious and conservative due to its wide range. This should dampen enthusiasm, they say. Shares are up 3.5% at 141.70 euros. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0920 GMT - China's consumer-price index likely fell 0.5% on year in February, according to a poll of economists by The Wall Street Journal, ending 12 months of tepid price growth. Although the decline is partly due to a high base, economists say it suggests that even after the recent stimulus pivot, policymakers have much to do to convince cautious households to spend more. The decline in China's factory-gate prices likely eased to 2.1% on year last month, improving from January's 2.3% drop, according to the poll. China's National Bureau of Statistics is slated to release the data on Sunday. (singaporeeditors@dowjones.com)

0915 GMT - Bitcoin falls, even after President Trump signed an executive order Thursday to create a strategic bitcoin reserve and an additional stockpile of other digital assets. Cryptocurrencies are lower as the bitcoin reserve and stockpile will be capitalized by forfeited assets already owned by the government rather than by any new funding, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. The news came ahead of Friday's White House crypto summit. Bitcoin falls 1.3% to $88,237, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0912 GMT - Thailand's inflation staying within the central bank's 1%-3% target range for three months is likely a temporary phenomenon, says Barclays economist Shreya Sodhani in a note. Inflation is likely to fall below the 1% lower bound by next month, she writes. Barclays continues to expect Thai inflation to average 1.1% this year, but see downside risks to its forecast. This is due to factors including lower global oil prices, she says. With inflation likely to be lower than the BOT's target range for most of the year, she expects monetary policy to stay focused on growth this year. Barclays continues to expect the central bank to deliver two more rate cuts this year. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0852 GMT - Yields on U.K. government bonds decline, tracking declines in eurozone government bond yields following this week's steep gains due to Germany's large-scale spending plans. Markets calm on Friday as investors take stock of recent moves and focus on U.S. jobs data due at 1330 GMT to guide investors on the possible path of future interest-rate cuts by the Federal Reserve. The European Central Bank cut interest rates by 25 basis points on Thursday, although it suggested that the pace of rate cuts could slow from here. The 10-year gilt yield falls 5 basis points to 4.668% as the equivalent Bund yield declines 6 basis points to 2.832%, Tradeweb data show. Ten-year gilt yields hit a seven-week high of 4.794% Thursday.(miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0803 GMT - A revival in demand for goods produced at German factories lingers on the horizon, Commerzbank economist Ralph Solveen says. Factory orders slumped at the start of the year, figures show Friday, quashing much of the optimism generated with the previous month's surge in orders. Orders seem to be stabilising, but a sustained rebound in demand is a long way off yet, Solveen says. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0801 GMT - The big miss in China's January-February export data shows that escalating U.S.-China trade tensions are biting, Barclays analysts say in a note. Barclays reckons the U.S.'s additional 20% tariffs on Chinese imports could reduce China's GDP growth by 0.8-1.0 percentage point. "The additional 20% tariffs will hit [an] already fragile labor market, which in turn will weigh on consumption," the analysts write. China's Commerce Ministry estimates that export-related jobs accounted for more than 20% of the country's total employment of 730 million in 2019-2022, they add. Meanwhile, they note that China's imports in the first two months posted the deepest contraction since mid-2023, reflecting weakened domestic demand in 1Q. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0755 GMT - The European Central Bank's more cautious stance on interest rate cuts combined with Germany's plans to increase fiscal spending provide a boost to the euro, Pepperstone strategist Michael Brown says in a note. It has created "something of a best of all worlds scenario" for the common currency, he says. The ECB on Thursday cut interest rates by 25 basis points, as expected, but hinted at the possibility of a slower pace of rate cuts going forward by saying monetary policy was becoming "meaningfully less restrictive," he says. The euro rises 0.4% to $1.0834. It hit a four-month high of $1.0854 Thursday, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0753 GMT - Eurozone government bond yields fall in early trade as investors keep absorbing the week's turmoil that lifted 10-year Bund yields to levels unseen since October 2023. "A memorable week is drawing to a close, allowing markets to take stock of the seminal changes in German fiscal politics as well as tariffs and European Central Bank [decision]," says Commerzbank Research's Michael Leister and Christoph Rieger in a note. Germany revealed a large-scale spending program, prompting the largest one-day move in Bunds since 1990 on Wednesday when 10-year Bund yields rose 30 basis points. German Bunds lead a fall in yields on Friday. The 10-year Bund yield falls 6.5 basis points to 2.832%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0735 GMT - The Philippine central bank is likely to cut its policy rate in April, OCBC economists say in a report. This expectation is driven by recent data, including weaker-than-expected 4Q GDP growth and low February inflation, they write. While inflationary pressures may rise from February's lows, they are expected to remain contained in the coming months, they add. The central bank has signaled that it is open to a cumulative 50bp rate cut this year, they say. "Given the increasingly volatile external backdrop, we will continue to assess the risks to additional rate cuts from BSP," they add. OCBC forecasts 25bp rate cut in April, followed by a hold for the remainder of the year.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0729 GMT - The dollar hovers near four-month lows as investors exercise caution ahead of the key U.S. nonfarm payrolls report later. The currency has come under recent pressure as concerns about slowing U.S. economic growth prompt markets to price in further Federal Reserve interest rate cuts. That means the payrolls data at 1330 GMT will take particular importance. Softer-than-expected jobs figures could further weaken the dollar, Swissquote Bank analyst Ipek Ozkardeskaya says in a note. The worse-case-scenario would be lower-than-expected payrolls combined with higher-than-expected wage growth as that would leave the Fed facing a slowing economy with limited room to give support, she says. The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 103.895 after hitting a low of 103.761 on Thursday. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0715 GMT - Being long U.S. Treasurys is still rewarding considering the risks, particularly with tariff headlines not going away anytime soon, says Pepperstone's Michael Brown in a note. In addition, growth worries subsequently are likely to continue to intensify, particularly as government spending cuts in the U.S. increasingly take effect, the senior research strategist says. Friday's potential drivers include February's nonfarm payrolls data at 1330 GMT. The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield last trades at 4.255%, down 3 basis points, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0700 GMT - The term premium has further 15-20 basis point potential to rise in German Bunds in 2025, with the 10-year yield likely reaching 3%, Societe Generale's rates strategists say in a note. However, for the 10-year Bund yield to go decisively above 3% would require hawkish repricing of the European Central Bank's interest-rate path, they add. Investors sold German bonds massively this week, pushing the 10-year yield to levels unseen since October 2023, in the wake of Germany's large-scale fiscal spending program. Societe Generale's strategsits prefer tactical, or short-term, short positions in Bunds, targeting a rise of 10-year yields closer to 3%. Having touched an intraday high of 2.933% on Thursday, the 10-year Bund yield ended at 2.789%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0707 GMT - Malaysian banks are likely to see moderate profit growth in 2025, supported by steady loan expansion and benign provisioning costs, RAM Ratings says in a note. Net interest margins are likely to remain stable as banks manage funding costs with policy rates remaining unchanged, it says. Loan growth is projected at 5.5%, the same pace as 2024, it reckons. While Malaysia's GDP is forecast to grow 4.0%-5.0%, compared with an expansion of 5.1% last year, risks from impending subsidy reforms and global uncertainties may dampen sentiment, it adds. RAM Ratings forecasts banks asset quality to remain strong, with impaired loan ratios at historical lows. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0555 GMT - BDO Unibank's earnings growth is likely to be supported by loan volumes, SB Equities' Katrine Eunice Dolatre says in a research report. The Philippine bank's income growth prospects are expected to be primarily be volume-driven, aided by branch expansion--targeting 100-120 new locations this year--and ongoing investments in productivity, the analyst says. The brokerage maintains its 2025-2026 gross loan forecasts for BDO Unibank at roughly 12%, in line with the bank's low-teens growth guidance for 2025. It keeps a buy rating on the stock but lowers the target price to PHP181.20 from PHP186.40 to reflect factors including a higher risk-free rate. Shares are 1.4% higher at PHP157.20. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0212 GMT - Singapore banks are likely to face a challenging year ahead, say RHB Research analysts in a report. Earnings could stay flat this year given the high base effects, they write. Banks could post a modest rise in net interest income and a moderation in noninterest income, due to the high base of other noninterest income, they say. However, fee growth should remain healthy, the analysts say. "With operating income growth slowing, we expect [Singapore] banks to keep a tighter rein on cost resulting in flat jaws," they say. RHB maintains an overweight rating on Singapore's banking sector.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

1741 GMT - Spanish banks may face some temporary headwinds in the short-term due to competition in lending and deposits, Deutsche Bank says in a research note. This comes after a period of outperformance driven by resilient net interest income, a more stable interest-rate outlook and a more benign environment supporting activity in the country, analyst Alfredo Alonso and associate Atul Hanamante write. "Despite encouraging 4Q24 results and a broadly positive 2025 outlook, the somewhat weaker 1H25 could potentially drive investors to take a more cautious stance," they say. DB cut its ratings on Sabadell and Unicaja to hold from buy and lowers CaixaBank to sell from hold. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

1608 GMT - Now that the ECB sees its policy as much less restrictive than in the past, the debate is shifting to when it will stop cutting rates, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says in a note. The debate is starting with rates at 2.5% and not 2.0%, analysts write, adding that this is a good environment for European banks. KBW's earnings forecast are based on the ECB's deposit rate being 2% by the summer and remaining stable until 2027. The shape of the yield curve is starting to change this week. "More banks have hedged interest rates, paying away the variable rate and receiving the fixed rates at roughly a five-year duration. Therefore, while the policy rate is important, the level of yield a bank can receive at five years or beyond is increasingly important," they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

2317 GMT - Australia's filling-station operators and supermarkets face earnings headwinds from the availability elsewhere of cheaper illicit tobacco products, according to Jefferies analysts. They write in a note that recent earnings reports from Viva Energy, Ampol, Coles and Woolworths show tobacco sales falling by between 15% and 26%. They point out that, while gross margins on tobacco sales are low, there are little associated costs, so a drop in sales implies material operating deleverage. They think that Viva likely experienced a 12% drag on December-half Ebit from the lower sales. The other three companies have better managed the decline, the analysts add. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1923 GMT - Oil futures post minor gains after being buffeted by news on the tariff front--President Trump agrees to put off tariffs on Mexico and Canada for another month--and reports of U.S. plans to tighten enforcement of sanctions against Iranian oil. Benchmark crudes settled at six-month lows yesterday after a string of losses on OPEC+ plans to start unwinding output cuts, tariff uncertainty and a big build in U.S. crude stocks. "While tariff adjustments may provide near-term relief, global demand uncertainties and rising supply levels suggest that crude oil prices could remain under pressure, limiting the potential for a sustained recovery," Exness market strategist Terence Hove says in a note. WTI edges up 0.1% to $66.36 a barrel and Brent rises 0.2% to $69.46 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1403 GMT - Oil futures are a bit higher after a four-session rout carried them to six-month lows on prospects of higher OPEC+ output, tariff concerns and a large build in U.S. crude stocks. "Still, crude oil storage remains well below the five-year averages and OPEC may be slow to raise production given the latest selloff in prices," Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. And while moves are afoot for a meeting between President Trump and Russia's Vladimir Putin, a peace deal to end the Russia-Ukraine war "could be many months away," he adds. WTI is up 0.6% at $66.72 a barrel, and Brent is up 0.4% at $69.57 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1044 GMT - Hunting PLC's outlook remains positive, particularly in the oil country tubular goods and subsea businesses, RBC Capital Markets analyst Victoria McCulloch says in a research note. The two businesses performed above management's 15% Ebitda target and attractive tendering opportunities this year should boost the order book, McCulloch says. The British energy-industry supplier posted 2024 results that are in line with consensus expectations. Shares trade 4.4% lower at 293.50 pence. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

data.title: China's Consumer Prices Likely Fell in February, WSJ Poll Shows — Market Talk
data.description: China's consumer-price index likely fell 0.5% on year in February, according to a poll of economists by The Wall Street Journal, ending 12 months of tepid price growth. Although the decline is partly due to a high base, economists say it suggests that even after the recent stimulus pivot, policymakers have much to do to convince cautious households to spend more. The decline in China's factory-gate prices likely eased to 2.1% on year last month, improving from January's 2.3% drop, according to the poll. China's National Bureau of Statistics is slated to release the data on Sunday. (singaporeeditors@dowjones.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0631 GMT - Vale Indonesia's new nickel-ore sales initiatives are expected to be a key catalyst in 2025, Nomura's Ahmad Maghfur Usman and Verdhana Sekuritas analysts say in a research report. The nickel miner has disclosed improved feasibility for its upstream mine and high-pressure acid leach projects, with a planned gross investment of US$8.6 billion, the analysts note. Ore sales could drive a three-year revenue CAGR of 29%, according to the brokerage's estimates. The company's limonite ore will supply four new HPAL facilities set to come online by late 2026. Nomura upgrades the stock's rating to buy from neutral, maintaining a target price of IDR4,000.00. Shares are 10.6% higher at IDR3,140.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0401 GMT - Hyundai Steel may get an earnings boost from China's steel output reduction and stimulus efforts, NH Investment & Securities analysts Rhee Jae-kwang and S.Y. Lee write in a note. The South Korean steelmaker can benefit from a possible steel-sector recovery, as China's economic planning agency earlier this week said it would mandate steel production cuts to restructure the sector, the NH analysts say. The agency has yet to give specific reduction targets, but markets expect China to cut its steel output by 150 million tons by 2030, they note. NH raises its target price for the stock by 25% to KRW40,000 and keeps a buy rating. Shares are 8.0% higher at KRW31,650. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0244 GMT - SK IE Technology may report a narrower-than-expected loss this year as it secures more contracts, including a 291.40 billion won deal in February, Nomura analyst Cindy Park writes in a note. The South Korean lithium-battery separator maker's shipments are forecast to rise 97% in 2025 but fall short of helping it break even, Park says. She expects the company's annual net loss at 74 billion won, compared with her earlier projection of 107 billion won, after factoring in new contract wins and still uncertain electric-vehicle demand. Nomura cuts its target price on the stock by 25% to KRW30,000 and keeps a neutral rating. Shares are 0.2% higher at KRW27,100. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0229 GMT - Iron ore prices are higher in early Asia trade. China's economic planning agency has said it would mandate steel production cuts, ANZ Research analysts write in a note, adding that no specifics were given on the volume of cuts in the sector. Some state media reports indicate that steel mills in Shandong have been instructed to cut production by around 4 million tons this year, ANZ says. The weak domestic market has also led Chinese steel mills to target the export market, which has weighed on international prices, they say. The most-traded iron-ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange gains 0.1% at CNY773.5/ton. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0058 GMT - Jefferies adds Barrick Gold to its North American shopping list for copper--an unconventional call, it acknowledges, given Barrick makes most of its money from the gold it mines. Analysts at the bank say they expect Barrick's attributable copper production to double to more than 450,000 metric tons annually and contribute roughly 25% of revenue later this decade. Copper accounts for about 10% of its revenue today. Barrick joins Capstone Copper, Ivanhoe Mines and Hudbay Minerals on Jefferies's list of preferred North American copper miners. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2253 GMT [Dow Jones]--Improvements in global softwood pulp market conditions are expected to continue into the second quarter, Canfor Pulp said, "as global supply dynamics adjust to new hardwood capacity and as pulp producer inventories normalize." However, the company said it is facing persistent challenges associated with the availability of economic fiber in British Columbia. "On the demand side, steady Chinese demand is anticipated to absorb these changes in supply," the company added. Canfor Pulp also said it is monitoring the trade situation between Canada and the U.S., with mitigation plans under way to mostly offset the impact of tariffs on U.S. shipments. The company said it is "well-positioned to respond to actual and potential tariffs." (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

2349 GMT - Rio Tinto has no need to raise equity, say Citi analysts Paul McTaggart and Ephrem Ravi. "So let's move on," they say in a client note. Rio Tinto's recent comments about a potential equity raise caught the market off guard and prompted a debate over whether the miner was seeking to rebalance shares in its dual-listed structure, strengthen its balance sheet, or have greater flexibility, say McTaggart and Ravi. "Even under downside scenarios, we think that Rio's balance sheet can handle commodity price volatility without new equity," they say. And, "if management believes Rio's shares are cheap, why dilute shareholders now?" Citi has a neutral rating at A$130 target on Rio Tinto. The stock is up 0.3% in Sydney at A$115.30. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2157 GMT - West African Resources' share price may have shot up 19% in two days after its annual result beat expectations, but Barrenjoey says the miner remains its top stock pick among West African gold companies. "One year forward earnings multiples are still eye watering," says analyst Richard Knights. The bank assumes West African Resources offers a free cash flow yield of 32% in 2026. "If Kiaka is delivered in the September quarter of 2025 as expected, we don't believe this discount will persist, even considering the market's concerns around sovereign risks in Burkina Faso," Barrenjoey says. Its price target rises 3.9%, to A$2.65/share. West African Resources ended Thursday at A$2.11. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0858 GMT - Deutsche Post's guidance reassures as it has been set at a manageable level, Deutsche Bank analyst Andy Chu writes. The bank says that since roughly this time last year it had been concerned about the risk to management guidance and the lack of a turn in the freight cycle. "Today's results allow us to pivot our view as guidance has been de-risked and re-based down to more achievable levels." Full-year EBIT guidance is in line with the bank's estimates and the medium-term guidance now doesn't try to predict economic cycles, Chu says. The bank upgrades Deutsche Post to buy from hold and raises its target price to 50 euros from 36 euros. Shares fall 2.4% to 43.22 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0825 GMT - Deutsche Post's investment case has been strengthened by management actions to contain costs and boost growth, JPMorgan analysts write. The company delivered strong fourth-quarter EBIT, derisked forward guidance by targeting an EBIT level it is confident in reaching despite macro uncertainties, and outlined a cost-improvement plan. "While share price performance year to date has been strong, partly driven by sentiment around German equities, we think the fact that management is demonstrating it is taking action to ensure earnings growth resumes, will help the investment case." JPMorgan lifts its price target on the stock to 50 euros from 42.50 euros and reiterates an overweight rating. Shares fall 1.8% to 43.48 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0739 GMT - Ninebot is poised to strengthen its dominance in the high-end intelligent electric two-wheeler market, Daiwa Capital Markets analysts say in a research report. Initiating coverage with a buy rating and a target price of CNY70.00, the brokerage projects the company's market share in China's high-end segment will reach around 36% by 2027. Analysts cite the company's leadership in intelligent technology, strong brand awareness among young consumers and ongoing nationwide expansion of its distribution network. Daiwa forecasts EPS CAGR of 37% for Ninebot over 2025-2027. Shares closed 2.1% higher at CNY60.39. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0248 GMT - Airports of Thailand faces greater hit to FY 2025 earnings from the delay in concession payments, UOB Kay Hian analysts say in a research report. The Thai company this week clarified that King Power Duty Free's concession payment will be postponed from September 2024 to April 2025 and King Power Selection's concession payment will be delayed from September 2024 to February 2025. Management at its 1Q FY 2025 analysts' meeting said that KPD and KPS had requested to delay concession payments from August 2024 to February 2025. These changes could have an estimated THB2.1 billion negative impact on AOT's FY 2025 concession revenue, the analysts say. The brokerage cuts the stock's target price to THB49.00 from THB52.00 with unchanged hold rating. Shares closed at THB40.50 on Thursday. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0019 GMT - Napier Port measures up well against its chief listed rival in New Zealand for stock pickers, according to Forsyth Barr. "Napier Port is trading at a 50% discount to its larger sector peer, Port of Tauranga, which presents an attractive entry opportunity, not least given a robust medium-term earnings growth outlook," analyst Andy Bowley says. Napier Port this week said it's tracking toward the top end of its FY 2025 Ebitda guidance range. It also reiterated a key goal for return on capital. Napier Port is down 2.2% today, amid broad-based weakness among NZ stocks. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1642 GMT - America's Car-Mart sold more cars in its recent quarter than it did a year earlier, despite operating in what CEO Doug Campbell says on a call with analysts is an uncertain market, in which used car affordability is a major concern among consumers. "The work that we've done as a company, by centralizing certain functions, finding new sources of inventory and tightening underwriting standards, will help us navigate the environment more effectively," he says. The company feels good heading into the seasonally-strong spring selling season, Campbell says, having added inventory and fine tuned its marketing strategy, while continuing to pursue opportunities to improve deal structures. Shares rise 18% following a better-than-expected F3Q. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1407 GMT - The early read suggests tariff front-running and a weak loonie supported Canadian trade flows in January, but looking ahead trade is expected to weigh heavily on growth as long as tariffs are in place even if a weaker Canadian dollar can absorb some of the hit, BMO Economics' Shelly Kaushik says. In volume terms, Canadian exports rose 3.4% on-month in January while imports were up 1.5%, suggesting a solid boost to January GDP growth, the economist says. "While it's still very early in the quarter, we suspect tariff front-running will support 1Q growth, with a sharp reversal expected starting in the next quarter, especially if the auto exemption [to U.S. tariffs] expires." (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1237 GMT - EU plans to become friendlier toward the auto industry, coupled with higher defense spending, could see shares in manufacturers move higher, Citi analysts say. Not only has the EU allowed a change in the carbon dioxide penalty regime, a new action plan provides a comprehensive industry-friendly strategy, although no new stimulus has been announced yet, they add. From being the most over-regulated auto region in the world, the EU is clearly looking to positively address key industry concerns, they say. Added to this is the potential for 800 billion euros of European defense spending, 500 billion euros in German defense/infrastructure spending, and possible U.S. tariff deals. "We think the EU autos rally may have legs. VW remains the best way to play this." (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1057 GMT - Lufthansa's increased capacity and higher load factor helped the German carrier group achieve revenue which exceeded expectations, Goodbody's Dudley Shanley says. The company increased capacity in the fourth quarter by 5.5% which, when combined with a load factor of 82.2%, helped revenue grow 8% in the quarter, the analyst says in a note. Lufthansa said that it expects to expand the seating capacity of its passenger airlines by around four percent, and that revenue will increase as a result. This leads Goodbody to expect the company to achieve around 2 billion euros in operating profit this year. The company made 1.645 billion in adjusted EBIT last year. Shares trade 7.4% higher 7.72 euros.(pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

1019 GMT - Air France-KLM's result for the end of last year is proof the Franco-Dutch carrier has shrugged off the negative effect of the Olympic games in Paris, Barclays analysts say in a research note. That said, political challenges in France and the Netherlands remain, but consensus expectations should rise as the company said it expected a 300 million euro improvement to EBIT this year, the analysts say. They add that Air France-KLM's guidance evokes confidence as the group saw a solid recovery in the last quarter of 2024. Air France's operating profit in the quarter improved by 324 million euros to 302 million euros, while Dutch partner KLM's operating profit improved by 69 million euros to 51 million euros. Shares trade 22% higher to 11.13 euros. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

0957 GMT - Deutsche Lufthansa encourages with its adjusted earnings outlook for this year, Davy Research analysts Stephen Furlong and Ava Costello say in a research note. After the company's performance improved in the second half, the German carrier group says that it expects its adjusted EBIT for 2025 to be significantly higher than the 1.645 billion euros it achieved last year. The result was slightly ahead of consensus expectations of 1.585 billion euros. The estimate comes as the company says it expects its turnaround plan to contribute 2.5 billion euros in gross profit by 2028. "Further execution of the turnaround programme should be a turning point for the group," the analysts say. Shares trade 6.3% higher to 7.64 euros.(pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

0938 GMT - Air France-KLM's guidance for this year provides room for consensus expectations to increase, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a research note. The Franco-Dutch carrier reported fourth-quarter EBIT that was well ahead of both company consensus at 160 million euros and Deutsche Bank's estimate of 188 million, the brokerage says. On a divisional basis, the beat was driven by the company's network airlines as well as the performance of its cargo business, the analysts say. However, the beat to the company's operating profit was offset by expenses, which led to a net loss for the quarter, the analysts say. Shares trade 17% higher to 10.65 euros. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

data.title: UK's Serica Energy in talks for possible combination with EnQuest
data.description:
UK's Serica Energy said on Friday that it is in talks regarding a possible combination with EnQuest as the London-listed companies target increased scale and diversification.

Shares in Serica and EnQuest were trading 9.4% and 5.5% higher, respectively, by 0914 GMT.

Serica said the potential deal will be structured as an all-share offer by EnQuest, and will involve a return of capital to existing Serica shareholders.

Serica shareholders would hold a majority of the shares in the combined company with shares listed on the ESCC market of the London Stock Exchange, Serica said in a statement.

EnQuest has time until 5 p.m. UKT on April 4 to make a firm offer for Serica or walk away.

data.title: Stocks slip amid US trade policy uncertainty, bond selloff eases
data.description:
Key points:
  • European stocks down 0.9%
  • Bond sell off abates
  • Euro heading for best week since 2009
  • Confusion over Trump's tariffs leaves investors on tenterhooks
  • Focus on US non-farm payrolls data, Powell's speech

Stocks in Europe and Asia slipped on Friday, with investors still edgy at the end of a week marked by confusion over U.S. trade policy and a global rise in borrowing costs, although a steep selloff in bonds abated.

European stocks fell 0.9%, on track for a weekly loss after 10 straight weekly gains, with luxury stocks and retailers (.SXRP) weighing heavily.

MSCI's broadest index of Asia-Pacific shares outside Japan (.MIAPJ0000PUS) lost 0.6%, with Japan's Nikkei falling to a six-month low.

Still, a sharp selloff in euro zone government bond markets triggered by Germany's plans for a huge spending package showed signs of tapering. After the biggest two-day fall in Bunds since the 1970s, the benchmark 10-year bond yield (DE10YT=RR), which moves inversely to prices, fell 5 basis points to 2.84%.

The euro, boosted by the rising euro zone borrowing costs, was set for its best week since 2009.

Financial markets were focused on a U.S. non-farm payrolls report and a speech from Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, due later in the day, which could provide more clarity on the outlook for interest rates in the world's biggest economy.

Wall Street has this week faced headwinds from a darkening outlook for U.S. growth and uncertainty over President Donald Trump's tariff policies, with the Nasdaq on Thursday confirming a correction since peaking in December.

Trump on Thursday suspended tariffs of 25% he had imposed this week on most goods from Canada and Mexico, the latest twist in a fluctuating trade policy that has whipsawed markets.

"With this being a delay rather than a lasting exemption and with reciprocal tariffs also expected to be announced after April 2, this leaves plenty of lingering tariff uncertainty," Deutsche Bank analysts wrote.

U.S. stock futures regained some lost ground, with Nasdaq futures rising 0.3% and S&P 500 futures adding 0.2%.

RISK OFF

Overall, however, the mood was risk off.

Chinese bluechip stocks fell 0.5%. Data on Friday showed China's imports unexpectedly shrank in January and February, while exports lost momentum, as escalating U.S. tariff pressures cast a shadow over the recovery in the world's second-largest economy.

"This slowdown comes before any substantial hit from tariffs, which will almost certainly lead to sharp falls in shipments to the U.S. before long," said Julian Evans-Pritchard, head of China economics at Capital Economics.

Government bonds elsewhere extended their selloff, though. The 10-year Japanese government bond (JGB) yield (JP10YTN=JBTC) rose 1.5 basis points to 1.53%, its highest level since June 2009.

Investors sold riskier currencies such as the Australian dollar , which fell as much as 0.5% to $0.62995. Perceived safe havens were in demand, with the yen up 0.5% to 147.81 against the dollar.

The Swiss franc struck a three-month top of 0.88125 against the dollar. Gold hovered near a record high at $2,922.75 an ounce .

The euro was set for its largest weekly gain since 2009 at 4.3%. It last traded 0.6% higher at $1.084.

The European Central Bank on Thursday cut interest rates again but warned of "phenomenal uncertainty," including the risk of rising inflation due to trade tensions and defence spending. (0#ECBIRPR)

Risk-sensitive bitcoin fell a smidgeon to $88,439. Trump signed an executive order on Thursday to establish a so-called strategic bitcoin reserve, built using tokens already owned by the U.S. government that were forfeited during criminal or civil asset forfeiture proceedings.

data.title: Singapore Forex Reserves Largest in Nearly 3 Years
data.description:
Foreign exchange reserves in Singapore increased to SGD 511.63 billion in February 2025, the largest value in almost three years, from SGD 510.56 billion in the previous month.

The rise was driven by increases in gold & foreign exchange (SGD 501.49 billion vs SGD 500.45 billion in January) and SDRs (SGD 8.45 billion vs SGD 8.37 billion).

Meanwhile, IMF reserve position decreased slightly (SGD vs 1.69 billion vs SGD 1.74 billion).

In February 2024, the reserves stood at SGD 480.48 billion.

data.title: Flughafen Zuerich shares dip after 2025 outlook disappoints
data.description:
** Shares in Swiss airport Flughafen Zuerich fall around 4% after it forecast stagnant core profit and higher capital expenditure in 2025

** The group's 2025 EBITDA is expected to be stagnant at the same level as in 2024 (CHF 733 million ($835.14 million)), whereas analysts at Vontobel had expected EBITDA growth in line with revenues

** Capex, projected at CHF 300-350 million at the Zurich site and CHF 300 million abroad is above consensus expectations of CHF 441 million, broker Jefferies says

** Analysts at Jefferies and Vontobel also point to delays at the group's Noida Airport project in India, now expected to ramp up at the end of Q2 instead of in April as previously announced

** The shares are on track for their worst day since April 2024

($1 = 0.8777 Swiss francs)

data.title: News Highlights: Top Energy News of the Day - Friday at 4 AM ET
data.description:
Oil snaps 4-day losing streak but remains stuck below $70 a barrel

Oil futures finished with small gains Thursday, snapping a four-day losing streak.

U.S. Natural Gas Futures Fall on Storage Miss

U.S. natural gas futures snapped a three-day winning streak as warmer weather contributed to a smaller-than-expected storage draw of 80 Bcf last week.

Harbour Energy Shares Sink to Record Low After Profit Hit by U.K. Windfall Tax

The oil-and-gas producer swung to a loss in 2024 as the energy profits levy continued to drag on results and its CEO described the U.K. as a difficult fiscal and regulatory environment in which to operate.

Sunnova Bondholders Engage Advisers Following KKR Deal, Going-Concern Warning

A group of bondholders in Sunnova Energy International has organized and hired legal counsel after the rooftop solar panel company issued a going concern notice and said it raised financing from KKR.

U.S. Crude Oil Stockpiles Rise More Than Expected

U.S. crude oil inventories rose by 3.6 million barrels last week, which was more than expected, resuming an upward trend after a surprise drawdown the week before.

Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on Hunting PLC, Harbour Energy, oil futures and more in the latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities.

DTEK in Talks With U.S. Suppliers to Boost Gas Supply to Europe

Ukraine's largest private energy company is in discussions with U.S. suppliers to bring more natural gas to Europe as part of a broader expansion, its CEO said in an interview.

Nuclear Power's Revival Is Here. What Do You Do With All the Radioactive Waste?

Tons of radioactive refuse go into temporary storage every year, and new efforts to store nuclear waste away from reactor sites are getting pushback.

Oil Outlook Clouded by OPEC+ Output, Trade Tariff Risks

Higher-than-expected supply and a potential demand hit due to softer U.S. activity and escalating tariffs pose downside risks to its oil price forecast, said Goldman Sachs.

U.S. Refiners Import Lots of Oil. Trump's Tariffs Spell Trouble.

Supplies from Canada and Mexico are at the heart of the U.S. oil industry.
